THE PRICE OF CONCENTRATED COMPETITION

Christopher H. Schmitt Investigative Group International April 1999
Investigative Methods

An analysis of Nasdaq trading records reveals investors incur higher costs when trading is concentrated among fewer dealers, impacting millions of investors.

Chris Schmitt's investigation into Nasdaq trading practices while at the San Jose Mercury News explored how concentrated competition among dealers on Nasdaq impacted investor trading costs. By analyzing Nasdaq trading records using the Trade and Quote database, the report revealed that investors were being burdened with sharply higher costs when competition among dealers waned. The data showed that reduced competition led to nearly twice the transaction costs of better competitive scenarios, potentially costing the investing public hundreds of millions annually.

Despite Nasdaq's skepticism, the investigation highlighted market performance issues and stretched computer-assisted reporting into areas typically sparse in data.
